title: Cultural and Spiritual Care
subtitle: The nurse provides culturally and spiritually sensitive psychiatric care — assess beliefs, stay nonjudgmental, use interpreters, and tell cultural beliefs from symptoms.

patient.name: Cultural / Spiritual Care
patient.admitDate: The nurse provides culturally sensitive care.

nursesNote: The nurse provides culturally and spiritually sensitive psychiatric care. The nurse assesses the patient's beliefs and practices rather than assuming, individualizes care, and avoids stereotyping. A nonjudgmental approach that does not impose the nurse's own values is essential, along with respecting dietary needs, prayer, rituals, and modesty. A professional interpreter is used for language barriers, not family members or children. Importantly, the nurse distinguishes culturally normal beliefs or spiritual experiences from psychiatric symptoms, since what is normal in one culture (for example, certain spiritual practices or beliefs) is not a delusion; the nurse clarifies with the patient and family when unsure. Spiritual support such as a chaplain is offered when the patient wishes. The nurse stays self-aware of personal biases and treats every patient with dignity to build trust.
providerOrders: Assess cultural and spiritual beliefs and needs
Provide nonjudgmental care without imposing personal values
Respect dietary needs, rituals, prayer, and modesty
Use a professional interpreter, not family
Distinguish cultural beliefs from psychiatric symptoms

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about culturally sensitive care?

opts.text: Assess beliefs rather than assuming
Stay nonjudgmental and avoid imposing values
Use a professional interpreter for language barriers
All members of a culture are the same
Some beliefs are culturally normal, not delusions
Respect dietary and religious practices
Clarify unfamiliar beliefs with patient/family
Offer spiritual support if the patient wishes
Family or children make the best interpreters
Be aware of your own biases
Respect modesty and personal space
A culturally normal belief is not a symptom

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order caring for a patient from a different culture

opts.text: Assess cultural and spiritual needs
Arrange a professional interpreter if needed
Clarify beliefs vs symptoms
Accommodate practices and preferences
Offer spiritual support and document
Asking the patient about their practices
Assuming beliefs based on ethnicity
Using a professional interpreter
Labeling a cultural belief as a delusion
Accommodating a prayer schedule
Imposing the nurse's own beliefs

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient describes a belief the nurse is unsure about

opts.text: Stay calm and nonjudgmental
Ask the patient/family about its meaning
Determine if it is a cultural norm or a symptom
Respect and accommodate if it is cultural
Document and inform the team
Asking about cultural and spiritual needs
Using the patient's child to interpret
Respecting dietary and religious practices
Calling a normal cultural belief a delusion
Offering a chaplain when desired
Imposing your own values on the patient
Clarifying unfamiliar beliefs with the patient
Stereotyping based on background
